title: Dog Eye Clouded Over White: Triage and Common Causes
meta_desc: If your dog's eye is clouded over white, it could be cataracts or an injury. Learn the urgency level and how a photo helps your vet triage.
content_html:
What is a White Clouded Eye?

When a dog's eye appears white or cloudy, it typically indicates a change in the transparency of the cornea or the lens. This can be caused by various factors, including cataracts (protein buildup in the lens), nuclear sclerosis (normal aging of the eye), or corneal edema (swelling due to injury or disease). Because the eye is a delicate organ, any change in clarity can impact your dog's vision and quality of life.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for a white, cloudy eye is Medium. While conditions like nuclear sclerosis develop slowly and are not painful, other causes like glaucoma or corneal ulcers can lead to permanent blindness if not treated quickly. If the cloudiness is accompanied by redness, squinting, or excessive tearing, you should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within 24 to 48 hours.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, high-resolution photo of your dog's eye is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photo allows us to see the exact location of the cloudiness—whether it is on the surface of the eye or deep within the pupil. It also helps us identify surrounding signs of inflammation or trauma, allowing our team to determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen by a doctor.

faqs.question: Does cloudiness always mean my dog is blind?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While cataracts can significantly block vision, other conditions like nuclear sclerosis only cause a slight change in focus and do not result in total blindness.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few weeks to see if it clears up?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Eye conditions can progress rapidly, and early intervention is the best way to save your dog's sight and prevent pain.

faqs.question: Should I use over-the-counter human eye drops?
faqs.answer: No. Never apply human eye medications to your dog unless specifically directed by a veterinarian, as some ingredients can be toxic or worsen certain types of eye injuries.
